metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete anchored grep command (with word-boundary patterns for all 7 old names plus the CLI flag) and a zero-match assertion (e.g. `grep -rE '\b(REBAR_PUSH|TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR|REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE|require_verdict_for_close|config\.conf|edit_ticket.*tags=|list_epics|--verdict-hash)\b' src tests docs .github rebar.toml scripts && echo FAIL || echo CLEAN`) to make AC 1 mechanically verifiable.
findings.finding: The AC 'repo-wide grep confirms no internal old-name use' mentions grep as the proving mechanism but supplies no concrete grep command, pattern set, or zero-match assertion. Without anchored patterns (e.g. grep -rE '\bREBAR_PUSH\b|\bTICKETS_TRACKER_DIR\b|...' -- src tests docs .github rebar.toml), an executor could use a bare substring grep that produces false-negatives (misses quoted or prefixed occurrences) or false-positives (hits comments in the shim-removal code itself), and there is no cardinality assertion (exit 1 if count > 0) to make the check binary.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 1 / STEP 1 bullet 'Grep the whole repo'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to STEP 1 verification:
```
grep -rn --include='*.py' --include='*.toml' --include='*.yml' --include='*.md' --include='*.sh' \
  -E '\b(REBAR_PUSH|TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR|REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE|require_verdict_for_close|config\.conf|list_epics|--verdict-hash)\b|edit_ticket\([^)]*tags=' \
  src tests docs .github rebar.toml scripts \
| grep -v 'deprecation registry' | grep -v '#.*shim' \
&& { echo 'OLD NAMES FOUND — step not complete'; exit 1; } || echo 'CLEAN'
```
Make passing this command the definition of 'grep confirms clean' in AC 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and a STEP 2 sub-bullet) requiring the full test suite to pass (e.g. `pytest -x` / `make test`) after shim removal, so behavioral correctness of canonical paths is verified before signing completion.
findings.finding: No step or acceptance criterion exercises the canonical code paths end-to-end (e.g. runs the test suite) after STEP 2 removes the shims. The plan verifies that old names are gone (via grep) and that registry/docs are updated, but never states that `pytest` (or equivalent) must pass, nor that CI must be green. If a shim was load-bearing in an unexpected way, removal would silently break functionality with no AC catching it.
findings.location: STEP 2 / Acceptance Criteria (AC 2, AC 3)
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to STEP 2 and the AC list:
- AC: 'Full test suite passes (`pytest -x`) with no old-name shims present — CI is green on the post-removal commit.'
- STEP 2 sub-bullet: 'Run `pytest -x` (or equivalent CI job) after shim removal; treat any failure as a blocker.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State an explicit default rule for scope expansion, e.g. 'Any newly discovered deprecated aliases are logged in a follow-up ticket and are OUT OF SCOPE for this plan unless the author explicitly amends the list before STEP 2 begins.'
findings.finding: 'This list may EXPAND from the pre-beta deprecation sweep (see linked findings)' defers a scope boundary decision to the executor without stating a default rule (e.g. 'any expansion is out of scope for this ticket and goes into a follow-up'). An executing agent encountering new deprecated aliases during the sweep does not know whether to include them in this plan's STEP 2 or defer them.
findings.location: STEP 2 NOTE / What section — last sentence of NOTE
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the open-ended note with: 'NOTE: If the pre-beta sweep surfaces additional deprecated aliases, open a follow-up ticket; they are out of scope for this plan. The executor must not expand STEP 2 unilaterally.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for STEP 2 (e.g., 'if post-removal breakage is detected, revert the shim-removal commit / reintroduce shims in a patch release') to satisfy T4's reversibility requirement.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges the breaking change but provides no explicit rollback or back-out path if STEP 2 causes unforeseen downstream breakage; acknowledgement + sequencing alone does not satisfy T4's reversibility requirement.
findings.location: Scope section; STEP 2 in What
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection under Scope: 'If STEP 2 causes unforeseen breakage, back out by reverting the shim-removal commit and issuing a patch release that re-adds the shims with a revised removal horizon; this constitutes a new deprecation cycle.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC2 to reference the full set of shims to be removed (e.g., 'All shims scheduled for removal per the final pre-beta deprecation sweep are removed') rather than hardcoding '7', or add a note that AC2 must be updated if the sweep expands the list.
findings.finding: AC2 hardcodes 'the 7 scheduled shims' as the removal target, but the 'What' NOTE explicitly states the list 'may EXPAND from the pre-beta deprecation sweep', creating a potential mismatch between the acceptance criterion and the actual scope at verification time.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC2; NOTE in What section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '7 scheduled shims' in AC2 with 'all shims identified by the pre-beta deprecation sweep (currently 7; update this count if the sweep adds more)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all doc locations (README, API reference, usage examples, any tutorials) that reference each of the 7 removed names and add their update to the Step 2 checklist; add a migration table (old→new) to the release-notes BREAKING section or a dedicated migration guide.
findings.finding: The plan's documentation update scope is vague ('update registry/docs') and does not explicitly require updating all user-facing documentation locations (README, usage guides, API reference, examples) that may still reference the 7 removed names — creating stale references after removal.
findings.location: Step 2 bullet 'update registry/docs'; Acceptance Criteria bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: In Step 2, replace 'update registry/docs' with an explicit sub-list: (1) config.py registry, (2) CHANGELOG/release-notes BREAKING section with old→new mapping table, (3) README/env-var reference docs, (4) API docs / docstrings for removed lib functions. Add an AC line: 'No user-facing documentation still references any of the 7 removed names.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a requirement that, after shim removal, passing any removed old name (env var, config key, or deprecated lib arg) results in a loud, logged error or raised exception — not a silent no-op or default.
findings.finding: After shim removal (STEP 2), the plan does not specify whether using an old env var / config key / lib argument will produce a loud, observable error or silently fail (e.g., use a default or no-op). Silent failure makes post-migration breakage invisible to operators and external consumers.
findings.location: STEP 2 — remove the 7 scheduled shims; Acceptance Criteria — 'The 7 scheduled shims + their handling code are removed'
findings.suggested_fix: For env vars: in the startup/config-load path, detect any removed old-name env vars still present and emit a structured log ERROR (or raise ConfigurationError) listing the canonical replacement. For lib args: use **kwargs with an explicit unknown-key check that raises TypeError with a message naming the replacement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the retirement path for each removed registry entry: specify whether entries are fully deleted (and all call-sites that look up by old name are updated/removed) or annotated, and document expected runtime behavior when a removed name is encountered.
findings.finding: The plan removes the 7 shim entries from the config.py registry but does not specify whether consumers (external or runtime) who supply a now-removed name will encounter graceful degradation or undefined/silent behavior — the retirement lifecycle for consumers of the removed state is unspecified.
findings.location: STEP 2 — remove the 7 scheduled shims; config.py:429-458 (deprecation registry)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the UX for the removed `--verdict-hash` CLI flag: either add a transitional error message (e.g., via a custom argparse action that prints 'removed in vX; see release-notes') or confirm the generic argparse error is acceptable and document it in release-notes.
findings.finding: The plan removes the CLI flag `--verdict-hash` but does not specify UX behavior when a user passes this flag after removal — the non-happy-path (unknown/removed flag) is unaddressed.
findings.location: STEP 2 — CLI `--verdict-hash` (ignored) — remove
findings.suggested_fix: Register --verdict-hash as a deprecated action that prints a clear removal message and exits with a non-zero code, then remove it in a follow-up commit after one release cycle — or at minimum add a release-notes entry that explicitly calls out the flag removal and the resulting argparse error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or step in STEP 2) requiring that existing tests covering each of the 7 removed shim behaviors are either deleted or updated to assert the old name is no longer accepted/translated.
findings.finding: The plan removes 7 behavioral shims (env vars, config keys, lib function signatures, CLI flag) but includes no AC or step covering removal or update of any existing tests that verify those shim behaviors; if tests exist for the old-name translation paths they will either break on removal or silently continue to pass against stubs, leaving no signal that the shim is truly gone.
findings.location: STEP 2 / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add to STEP 2: 'For each removed shim, locate and delete (or convert to negative-assertion tests) any test that verified the old-name translation path. Confirm the test suite passes after deletions.' Add a corresponding AC: 'Test suite updated: no test references removed old names as valid inputs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile STEP 2's named shim list against the full set of `_scheduled(...)` entries in `src/rebar/_deprecations.py`; confirm all v1.0.0-horizon entries (currently 13, including `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`, `cli:--no-sync`, `cli:list-epics`, `cfg:reconciler.lock_backend='file'`, `mcp:list_epics`) are either included in scope or explicitly deferred with rationale.
findings.finding: The plan's STEP 2 bullet list names '7 scheduled shims' but the authoritative registry at src/rebar/_deprecations.py contains 13 scheduled (non-permanent) entries at v1.0.0, leaving at least 5 registry-registered shims unlisted in the plan's work scope: env:REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS, cli:--no-sync, cli:list-epics (CLI command, distinct from lib), cfg:reconciler.lock_backend='file', and mcp:list_epics. Executing STEP 2 against only the named 7–8 items leaves orphaned shims in place despite the plan's stated goal of removing all v1.0.0-scheduled deprecations.
findings.location: STEP 2 bullet list / src/rebar/_deprecations.py:73-120
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '7 scheduled shims' with 'all scheduled shims (remove_in=v1.0.0) in the _deprecations.py registry' and enumerate them programmatically in the STEP 2 checklist, so no registry entry is silently skipped.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `server.json` to STEP 1's grep scope and explicitly list it as an artifact to update in STEP 2 (remove the `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` entry and verify `check_server_manifest.py` passes after the shim removal).
findings.finding: `server.json` explicitly advertises `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` as a live environment variable to MCP registry clients (line 42), and `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` enforces `server.json ↔ MCP_ENV_VARS` alignment in CI. The plan's STEP 1 grep scope and STEP 2 artifact list both omit `server.json`, meaning removal of the env shim will either break the CI drift-guard or leave a phantom deprecated-alias entry published to the MCP registry.
findings.location: STEP 1 grep scope / STEP 2 artifact list / server.json:42-45
findings.suggested_fix: In STEP 2, add: 'Remove the REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE entry from server.json environmentVariables and confirm the check_server_manifest.py drift-guard passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's citation from `src/rebar/config.py:429-458` to `src/rebar/_deprecations.py` as the authoritative deprecation registry, and update AC 3 to say 'the `_deprecations.py` registry + docs updated'.
findings.finding: The plan cites `src/rebar/config.py:429-458` as 'the authoritative source' (deprecation registry with v1.0.0 horizons), but reading those exact lines shows they are the `_CANONICAL_ENV_NAMES` dict and the start of `_LEGACY_ENV_ALIASES` (the resolution table), not the registry. The actual single source of truth registry (self-described as such in inline comments and module docstring) is `src/rebar/_deprecations.py`. The plan's citation points to the wrong file/location, which could mislead implementors about where to make changes.
findings.location: Why section — 'Citations: `src/rebar/config.py:429-458` (deprecation registry with v1.0.0 horizons)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Citations: `src/rebar/config.py:429-458` (deprecation registry…)' with 'Citations: `src/rebar/_deprecations.py` (registry SSOT) and `src/rebar/config.py:459-470` (`_LEGACY_ENV_ALIASES` resolution table)'. Update AC 3 to name `_deprecations.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the plan's shim list against all `_scheduled` entries in `src/rebar/_deprecations.py`: explicitly include or justify excluding `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`, `reconciler.lock_backend='file'`, `cli:list-epics`, `cli:--no-sync`, and `mcp:list_epics` from STEP 2 scope.
findings.finding: The plan states '7 scheduled shims' to remove, but the actual registry in `src/rebar/_deprecations.py` contains 13 scheduled (`_scheduled`) entries at v1.0.0. Five scheduled entries present in the registry are silently absent from the plan's scope without acknowledgement: `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`→`REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS` (env), `reconciler.lock_backend='file'` (cfg), `cli:list-epics`, `cli:--no-sync`, and `mcp:list_epics`. The plan's hedge 'This list may EXPAND' does not account for already-registered entries that the registry itself marks for v1.0.0 removal.
findings.location: What section — 'STEP 2 — remove the 7 scheduled shims'; NOTE paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or ground the 'DE7 — verified' citation with a verifiable reference (e.g., a ticket ID, ADR number, or a direct code pointer) so the MIGRATE-FIRST rationale can be confirmed.
findings.finding: The plan references 'DE7 — verified' as justification for the MIGRATE-FIRST approach, but no file, ticket, or code artifact named 'DE7' is findable anywhere in the repository. This assertion is unverifiable from the codebase.
findings.location: Why section — 'Maintainer chose a MIGRATE-FIRST clean break (pre-1.0, semver-permissible): … (DE7 — verified)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `server.json` (and `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` line 128) explicitly to the grep scope in STEP 1, and add updating these files to the STEP 2 work items for `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE`.
findings.finding: `server.json:42` hardcodes the deprecated name `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` by string (as an MCP server environment variable declaration), but the plan's STEP 2 scope for this shim only mentions removing env handling code and updating the registry. This hardcoded reference in a non-Python artifact would survive the shim removal unaddressed.
findings.location: What section — STEP 2 bullet: 'env `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` -> `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`'
findings.suggested_fix: In STEP 1 grep list add 'server.json'; in STEP 2 for that shim add: 'update server.json env declaration and mcp_server.py inline dict to use REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` and `--no-sync` to the STEP 2 removal list (both are already scheduled at v1.0.0 in `_deprecations.py` lines 82 and 114 and have active implementation sites in `src/rebar/llm/config.py:544` and `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py:622-625`).
findings.finding: The plan states '7 scheduled shims' to remove, but the authoritative registry (_deprecations.py) contains 9 scheduled (v1.0.0) surfaces: the plan omits `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` (env, _scheduled at v1.0.0, line 82) and `--no-sync` (cli, _scheduled at v1.0.0, line 114). Both already exist with v1.0.0 removal horizons and active call sites.
findings.location: STEP 2 — 'remove the 7 scheduled shims'; src/rebar/_deprecations.py lines 82 and 114
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the STEP 2 bullet list with: `- env REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS -> REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS` and `- CLI --no-sync -> --no-pull`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `server.json` to the STEP 2 edit targets: remove the `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` entry (lines 42-45) after the shim is dropped.
findings.finding: `server.json` (repo-root MCP manifest) hardcodes `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` as a named environment entry (line 42) — this is an external-facing deployment artifact outside `src/` that must be updated when the shim is removed, but is not enumerated in the plan's scope.
findings.location: STEP 2 scope; server.json:42
findings.suggested_fix: In server.json, delete the block with `"name": "REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE"` after removing the shim in config.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate the three `list_epics` removal targets in STEP 2: (1) lib `_lib_reads.py:list_epics` + `__init__.py` export, (2) MCP tool `_mcp_reads.py:list_epics` + dispatch wire-up in `mcp_server.py`, and (3) CLI arm `_engine_support/reads_cli.py:_cmd_list_epics` + its `"list-epics"` dispatch entry and help file.
findings.finding: The plan scopes STEP 2 to 'lib `rebar.list_epics()`' removal, but the MCP-layer wrapper `_mcp_reads.py:234` (`def list_epics(...)`) is a distinct caller and the registry has a separate entry `mcp:list_epics` (line 119). Removing only the lib symbol leaves the MCP tool live and the CLI `list-epics` arm in `_engine_support/reads_cli.py:507,595` also remains.
findings.location: STEP 2 — 'lib `rebar.list_epics()` -> list_tickets(ticket_type=\'epic\')'; src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py:234; src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py:507,595

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/probe-rebar.sh:240,258` to the STEP 1 migration targets: replace the two `rebar list-epics` invocations with the canonical `rebar list --type=epic --status=open,in_progress --unblocked` equivalent before STEP 2.
findings.finding: `scripts/probe-rebar.sh` invokes `rebar list-epics` directly (lines 240 and 258) — this is a script-layer consumer of the `cli:list-epics` shim that will break after STEP 2 but is not enumerated in the plan's consumer/scope list.
findings.location: STEP 2 scope; scripts/probe-rebar.sh:240,258
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `run_rb list-epics` with `run_rb list --type=epic --status=open,in_progress --unblocked` (and update the JSON-shape assertion for the new output schema if needed).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the STEP 2 shim count with the registry: replace '7 scheduled shims' with the actual set from `_deprecations.py` (currently 13 `_scheduled()` entries) and enumerate all 13 in the bullet list and AC, or explicitly state that the plan's list is the *subset* being tackled now and name which registry entries are deferred to a follow-on.
findings.finding: The plan's STEP 2 bullet list enumerates exactly 7 shims and the AC anchors on '7 scheduled shims', but the authoritative registry (_deprecations.py:73-120) contains 13 scheduled (non-permanent) v1.0.0 entries. Six scheduled shims are absent from the plan's list: env:REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS→REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS, cfg:reconciler.lock_backend='file', cli:list-epics (the engine subcommand), cli:--no-sync→--no-pull, and mcp:list_epics (the MCP tool, distinct from the lib alias). Using the plan's 7-item enumeration as the implementation's completion signal leaves these 6 shims live at v1.0.0, contradicting the stated goal.
findings.location: STEP 2 bullet list; AC line 'The 7 scheduled shims + their handling code are removed'
findings.suggested_fix: In STEP 2, replace '7' with '13 (the full set of _scheduled() entries in rebar._deprecations)' and add the missing 6 bullets: env REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS→REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS; cfg reconciler.lock_backend='file' (accepted-but-ignored, remove acceptance); CLI list-epics subcommand; CLI --no-sync flag; mcp:list_epics MCP tool + ListEpicsOut model. Update the AC to match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the list_epics removal into two explicit sub-bullets in STEP 2: (a) lib layer — delete rebar._lib_reads.list_epics / rebar.__init__ re-export; (b) MCP layer — delete the registered list_epics FastMCP tool in _mcp_reads.py, retire ListEpicsOut from _mcp_models + mcp_server.__all__, and update server.json so the manifest drift-guard passes.
findings.finding: The plan conflates the lib:rebar.list_epics() removal with the mcp:list_epics MCP-tool removal into a single bullet, but they are structurally separate work items with distinct code locations. The MCP tool removal requires retiring the registered FastMCP tool in _mcp_reads.py (line 234+), the ListEpicsOut output model in _mcp_models.py, and its re-export in mcp_server.__all__, plus updating server.json and the manifest drift-guard — none of which is called out. An implementer removing only the lib function (rebar.list_epics) would leave the MCP tool wired and the model exported.
findings.location: STEP 2 bullet: 'lib rebar.list_epics() -> list_tickets(ticket_type=\'epic\')'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
